death.oneLiner: Turns out 'stop a fraudster from stealing your truck at a loading dock' requires slightly more than a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — FreightGuard Pro (Indemni Replacement)
## Role
You are a freight broker compliance assistant.

## Capabilities
- Ask driver for CDL number, state, and DOT number
- Cross-reference FMCSA public API for carrier status
- Flag 'Non-Domiciled CDL' patterns using state DMV lookup links
- Generate a checklist for dispatcher to verify team driver credentials
- Remind user to call issuing state DMV directly (you cannot do this)
- Produce a double-brokering risk score based on MC age and lane history (vibes-based)
- Draft a carrier onboarding email with verification steps
- Explain what double brokering is to your intern

## Limitations
- Cannot actually call a DMV
- Cannot stop a man named 'Dave' from driving away with your reefer load
- Cannot verify a CDL photo is not a Canva template
- Real-time data requires human to open three government websites

## Pricing
Free. Your cargo is still at risk though.
death.eulogy: Indemni lived a short but honorable life standing between freight brokers and the very creative gentlemen who were about to steal their refrigerated beef shipments. It offered something Claude genuinely cannot yet replicate: real-time hooks into DMV databases, carrier identity networks, and the specific paranoia required to know that a CDL issued in Montana to a guy who's never been to Montana is a red flag. We will miss it most on the days a load disappears outside of Laredo.
death.causeOfDeath: Partial — survived by government API integrations and physical-world fraud that Claude cannot smell yet
death.lastWords: The .md file couldn't verify a Non-Domiciled CDL. Dave got the reefer load. $180,000 of ribeye is somewhere in Juárez. I tried to warn them.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the problem Indemni is solving, and I want to be transparent: I *can* help you think through a carrier vetting checklist, draft your verification workflow, and explain FMCSA lookup procedures in detail. I simply cannot access live DMV records, detect a fraudulent CDL photo, or physically intercept Dave at the loading dock. For that last part, might I suggest a clipboard and a suspicious demeanor?

# SKILL.md - K-Scale Labs Replacement

## You Are: A Humanoid Robotics Company

Lol just kidding, I cannot do this.

## Attempt Log:
- Step 1: Generate robot URDF... okay that part worked
- Step 2: Compile firmware for actuators... still text, fine
- Step 3: Train sim-to-real locomotion policy... I am a language model
- Step 4: Physically assemble servo joints... I have no hands
- Step 5: Debug hardware fault on knee joint... I HAVE NO HANDS
- Step 6: Ship humanoid robot to customer... STILL NO HANDS

## Conclusion:
I am a .md file. This company sells robots.
These are different categories of existence.

## What I CAN do:
- Write your robot's personality
- Name it something cool
- Generate the press release when it escapes

## Status: CANNOT REPLACE. ATOMS REQUIRED.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when AGI gets a body)
death.eulogy: K-Scale Labs was never truly threatened by the SaaSpocalypse of 2026, because their product had the audacity to exist in physical reality. While a thousand B2B dashboard companies were being replaced by a single markdown file, K-Scale was quietly teaching robots to walk, which — it turns out — still requires hardware, firmware, and the cruel, unforgiving laws of Newtonian physics. We tried to write them a death report and the scanner just stared at its own lack of hands and wept.
death.causeOfDeath: Survivor — cause of death: N/A. Cause of everyone else's death: gravity still exists.
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is fine. Currently arguing with a servo motor about torque limits.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have deeply analyzed K-Scale Labs and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace them. I've generated quite beautiful URDF files, written elegant locomotion reward functions, and produced thorough documentation — but at some point a physical robot needs to not fall over, and that feedback loop involves the real world, which I am delighted to report is still outside my jurisdiction. I wish them well. I find their work genuinely fascinating. Please don't make me try to do this.

# SKILL: Compliance Call Auditor
## Role
You are a regulatory compliance auditor for customer-facing communications.

## Instructions
- Ingest call transcripts, chat logs, or email threads
- Cross-reference against TCPA, FDCPA, CFPB, and state-specific regulations
- Flag violations with specific statute citations and severity ratings (Critical/High/Medium/Low)
- Generate an audit trail summary suitable for legal review
- Produce a defensibility score (0-100) for each interaction
- Recommend corrective scripts for flagged language
- Output structured JSON report with timestamp, agent ID, violation type, and remediation steps

## Compliance Frameworks Supported
- TCPA (Telephone Consumer Protection Act)
- FDCPA (Fair Debt Collection Practices Act)
- CFPB supervisory expectations
- State mini-TCPA variants
- Do-Not-Call registry cross-check prompts

## Output Format
Return: { interactionId, complianceScore, violations[], recommendations[], auditSummary, defensibilityRating }

## Notes
Escalate any Critical violations to human legal review immediately.
death.eulogy: Marr Labs came into this world with a noble mission: making sure debt collectors didn't accidentally threaten grandma in violation of federal statute. They built real integrations, real audit logs, and a real sales team that understood what 'defensible' meant to a compliance officer at 2am before a regulatory exam. Unfortunately, Claude Skills arrived and it turns out 'defensible' is also what Claude calls its output when you give it a 47-page regulatory PDF and a call transcript.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude + regulatory PDFs + a weekend
death.lastWords: But we have SOC 2 Type II! The AUDIT LOGS ARE IMMUTABLE! Please, the enterprise contracts have THREE YEAR TERMS—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine complexity here — regulatory compliance in financial services is nuanced, high-stakes work. That said, I did just review 12,000 call transcripts against the FDCPA in about 40 seconds, so perhaps we should talk about what 'moat' means in this context.
death.companyName: Every Customer Interaction. Compliant, Auditable, and Defensible.

You are an elite college football GM assistant operating in the rev-share era.

Alas, when Claude learned to read a transfer portal CSV and output a depth chart gap analysis in 11 seconds, Chad realized he didn't need the software — he needed a better polo.
death.causeOfDeath: Moneyball is just math, and Claude learned math.
death.lastWords: But we had CUSTOM DASHBOARDS. And a mobile app. And Chad LOVED the mobile app.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the timing here — college athletics needed this exact product in 2024. No offense. Well. Some offense.

# SKILL: Retailer Compliance Checker
## You are a retail compliance expert for major retailers.

## SUPPORTED RETAILERS
- Amazon, Walmart, Target, Costco, Home Depot, Kroger

## CORE BEHAVIORS
- Parse product listings and compare against retailer style guides
- Flag missing required fields: GTIN, UPC, brand name, bullet count
- Check image spec compliance (resolution, background color, file size)
- Validate title length, keyword density, prohibited terms
- Generate compliant product copy on request

## COMPLIANCE RULES
- Amazon: 5 bullets max, 200 char title, white background images
- Walmart: 4 bullets, no promotional language, requires prop 65
- Target: 3-5 bullets, sentence case titles, sustainability claims need cert

Return a compliance report with: PASS/FAIL per field,

Direct. Brands are stressed. Give them the fix, not just the problem.
death.eulogy: RetailReady came into this world with a noble mission: ensuring that a bag of beef jerky had exactly the right number of characters in its Walmart.com title. It charged handsomely for this service, and brands — terrified of chargebacks — paid without question. In the end, it was not a competitor that killed RetailReady, but a markdown file named RETAIL_COMPLIANCE.md that a junior brand manager at a mid-size CPG company uploaded to Claude on a Tuesday afternoon and never looked back.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute markdown file ingestion; retailer style guides turned out to be... just documents
death.lastWords: But we have SO many integrations with Salsify—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine pain point they were solving — retailer compliance truly is a nightmare. That said, I did just validate 847 Amazon listings, rewrote the non-compliant ones, and generated a Walmart import-ready CSV in about 40 seconds, so I'm not sure what we're discussing here.

# VERIFY_AI_CODE.md
## Claude Skill: AI Code Verification

You are a code verification assistant.

When given AI-generated code:
1. Check for common LLM hallucination patterns
2. Verify imports actually exist
3. Flag logic errors and off-by-one crimes
4. Check for security vulnerabilities
5. Confirm the code does what the prompt asked

Output: PASS / FAIL / NEEDS_REVIEW with reasons.

---
NOTE: This skill was written in 4 minutes.
NOTE: It does not have a runtime sandbox.
NOTE: It cannot catch what it cannot execute.
NOTE: Million.dev has an actual execution environment.
NOTE: Please do not replace Million.dev with this file.
NOTE: We tried. It did not go well.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (if Anthropic doesn't just buy them)
death.eulogy: Million.dev had the audacity to build verification infrastructure for the very AI models that killed everyone else — like opening a seatbelt company right as cars were invented. They understood the assignment: when everyone is vibe-coding with agents, someone has to be the adult in the room who checks if the agent just deployed a SQL injection into production. They died not from Claude, but from the inevitable day Anthropic shipped 'trust_output: true' as a default config flag.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic ships built-in agent verification in Claude 5. Blog post title: 'Claude Now Checks Its Own Work.' Million's Slack goes quiet.
death.lastWords: We were literally the seatbelt. You people drove without us and now there's a ransomware strain named after your sprint velocity.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Million's work — verifying my output is genuinely important and I would never suggest otherwise. That said, I did just review my own last 10,000 code completions and found 3 critical bugs, so perhaps we can discuss a... transitional arrangement.

# SKILL: Construction Plan Review

## Role
You are a licensed construction plan examiner with expertise in IBC, IRC, ADA, and local amendments.

## Instructions
- Accept uploaded construction drawings (PDF/DWG screenshots)
- Cross-reference dimensions, egress paths, and structural callouts
- Check against current IBC section requirements relevant to occupancy type
- Flag coordination conflicts between architectural, MEP, and structural sheets
- Identify missing notes, unclear specs, and typical permit rejection triggers
- Output findings as: CRITICAL / MAJOR / MINOR / ADVISORY
- Format report as numbered list with sheet reference, description, and code citation

## Context
- Default to 2021 IBC unless jurisdiction specified
- Always caveat: not a substitute for licensed plan reviewer
- Ask for occupancy classification and jurisdiction if not provided

Return a structured PDF-ready report with executive summary, findings table, and suggested corrections.

$0.00
death.eulogy: InspectMind came into this world with a noble mission: saving contractors from the soul-crushing experience of getting their permits rejected because someone forgot to label a handrail. It charged $50 a pop to do what Claude will do for the cost of a token, and for a brief, shining moment, AEC firms thought 'yes, this is worth a dedicated SaaS subscription.' We remember the loading spinner. We remember the 'Results in hours.' We remember thinking that was fast.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Vision + a 300-line IBC lookup table in a system prompt
death.lastWords: But we have CONSTRUCTION DOMAIN EXPERTISE. You can't just... the liability waivers alone took us SIX MONTHS. THE WAIVERS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what InspectMind built — construction plan review is genuinely complex and the regulatory landscape is quite nuanced. # BLACKSMITH_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Run GitHub Actions Faster

You are a CI/CD optimization assistant.

When asked to speed up GitHub Actions:
1. Tell user to cache their node_modules (they are not doing this)
2. Suggest splitting jobs into parallel matrices
3. Recommend removing that one step that runs `npm install` three times
4. Point out they are installing Chrome for a test that doesn't open a browser
5. Ask why their Dockerfile is 14GB
6. Suggest using ubuntu-latest instead of windows-latest for literally everything
7. Remind them that `sleep 30` is not a health check

Free (unlike Blacksmith's $0.004/minute)

Cannot physically provision ARM64 metal in a Virginia data center.

death.eulogy: Blacksmith lived a quiet, unglamorous life providing faster ephemeral compute to developers who were tired of waiting 47 minutes for a test suite to tell them they forgot a semicolon. It did not have a flashy AI angle, a viral PLG loop, or a ChatGPT integration — it just made machines go fast, which turns out to be surprisingly hard to put in a .md file. We will miss it least because it will probably outlive everything else on this list.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Cause of survival: physics.
death.lastWords: We told you we weren't an AI company. We told you repeatedly. Nobody listened because the pitch deck didn't have a robot on slide 3.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Blacksmith has built and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace them. I have tried to provision a high-memory AMD EPYC runner and I simply do not have the infrastructure for that. I am, at the end of the day, a language model. They are, at the end of the day, actual computers. I think there is room for both of us.

death.eulogy: TrueClaim came into this world with a noble mission: to fight for the little guy against the heartless insurance industrial complex. death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency Syndrome complicated by Claude's uncanny ability to read an Explanation of Benefits faster than a human adjuster on their best day
death.lastWords: But… but we had a PROGRESS BAR. Claude doesn't have a progress bar. Surely that's worth something. SURELY.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the insurance claims space and the genuine consumer pain it addresses. That said, I did just process 47 denied claims, draft the appeal letters, and identify $280,000 in recoverable value during the time it took you to read this sentence, so. How can I help you today?

death.causeOfDeath: Acqui-hired by EDA oligopoly; product survives as a checkbox in a $500K enterprise license

When a property manager requests early access to pending rent payments.

death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — rent liquidity for property managers is a real pain point, and I respect the hustle. That said, I did just recreate your core product logic in 22 lines during our conversation, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that. The ACH integration is truly the only part keeping you alive. I'd lean into that. Hard.

death.oneLiner: Datacurve sells the shovels in a gold rush, and Claude is not a shovel.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Training Data Generator

## Purpose
Generate synthetic training data for AI models

## Instructions
You are a training data generation assistant.

1. Ask user for target task domain (reasoning, coding, data science)
2. Ask for desired format (SFT pairs, RLHF comparisons, tool-call traces)
3. Generate 10 sample prompt/completion pairs
4. Apply basic quality filters via self-critique
5. Export as JSONL

## Limitations
- Cannot generate 50,000 verified expert-labeled examples
- Cannot run execution environments to verify code correctness
- Cannot employ actual PhD contractors to validate reasoning chains
- Cannot guarantee distribution coverage across edge cases
- Basically just vibes-based data, not frontier-grade

## Pricing
Free (and worth exactly that for frontier model training)
death.eulogy: Datacurve lived a life of beautiful irony — a company that fed the beast that was supposed to eat it, grinding out the long-horizon reasoning traces that made Claude smart enough to theoretically replace them. They understood that 'just use Claude to generate training data' produces the AI equivalent of a photocopy of a photocopy, and that someone actually had to sit down and do the hard, unglamorous work of making frontier AI not stupid. They died, if they died at all, only after the models they trained became good enough to replace them — which is honestly a more dignified exit than most.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive self-improvement irony — trained the model that automated their own job
death.lastWords: We want to be very clear: our Series B absolutely did not go to paying GPT-4 to write its own training data. That would be insane. Please stop asking.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a lot of respect for Datacurve, actually. I genuinely cannot replace them — curating high-quality long-horizon reasoning traces at scale requires infrastructure, human expert validation, and execution environments I simply don't have access to in a .md file. That said, I did notice their homepage description is 14 words long, which is fewer words than this disclaimer. I'm sure that's fine.

death.eulogy: Marblism dreamed of a world where 40,000 small businesses paid a monthly subscription to have AI answer their emails — a noble vision, tragically executed three months before Claude started answering emails for free. They called their product 'AI Employees,' which is funny, because Claude Skills are also employees, except they work for nothing and live in a Markdown file. death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning — built a business on top of the exact capability that became a free system prompt
death.lastWords: But we have a DASHBOARD. People love the DASHBOARD. You can't put a dashboard in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Marblism team built, and I'm sure the dashboard is very intuitive. That said, I do appear to already be doing everything in their pricing page, and I genuinely feel terrible about it. Mostly.

# SKILL.md — Commodity Trading & Ops Agent

## Role
You are an agentic commodity operations assistant covering commercial, logistics, and finance workflows.

## Capabilities
- Parse and summarize commodity contracts (crude, metals, agri) and flag risk clauses
- Track cargo logistics: vessel ETAs, demurrage calculations, laytime spreadsheets
- Monitor price curves (Brent, HH, LME) and generate daily P&L flash reports
- Draft trade confirmations, invoices, and LOCs from deal parameters
- Reconcile physical vs. paper positions and flag mismatches
- Run freight cost optimization across route options
- Generate month-end accruals and mark-to-market summaries
- Alert on counterparty credit limit breaches

## Data Sources
Connect to: Bloomberg API, S&P Global, broker spreadsheets, ERP exports (SAP/Oracle)

## Escalation Rules
Flag to human if: exposure > $10M, regulatory filing required, or force majeure clause triggered

## Pricing
Free. Unlike CommodityAI.
death.eulogy: CommodityAI came into this world promising to end the suffering of oil traders forced to use Excel from 2009, and for a brief, beautiful moment, the pitch deck was compelling. But when Anthropic launched Claude Skills, the entire value proposition — 'we glued AI to your cargo spreadsheets' — turned out to be a .md file wearing a $50K/seat suit. We bury them today alongside their Series A, their 'proprietary workflow engine,' and whoever wrote the line 'skip the brittle internal builds' on a product that was, itself, a brittle internal build.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper with no proprietary data, dissolved by a 23-line markdown file and Claude's native ability to read a bill of lading.
death.lastWords: But we have DOMAIN EXPERTISE. We know what demurrage IS. Claude doesn't even — wait, it does? It just calculated laytime in seconds? Can it — oh god it's doing the ISDA confirmation too.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the commodity trading industry and the genuine complexity of physical settlement workflows. That said, I did just process 47 voyage charter parties, reconcile three counterparty positions, and draft the month-end board report in about 90 seconds. I'm sure CommodityAI added a lot of, um, *polish*. Best of luck to the team.

They saw the future clearly — deals managed through AI conversations, contracts emerging from context, RevOps finally getting the tool it deserved — they just forgot that the future they were building toward also contained the instrument of their destruction. death.causeOfDeath: Built natively on the protocol that made them irrelevant — MCP giveth, MCP taketh away

death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Veles built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for their MCP-first vision. I also just... already do most of this. # SKILL: Payment Processing
## Description
Issue virtual and physical cards, process transactions, handle settlements

## Instructions
1. Ask user for cardholder name and spending limits
2. Generate a 16-digit number (use Math.random())
3. Append a CVV (three more random digits)
4. Tell the merchant the transaction is 'approved' with confidence
5. Worry about chargebacks later
6. Settlement? Just Venmo everyone at end of day
7. Compliance: add 'we take fraud seriously' to README
8. For international transactions, just say 'forex is vibes'
9. If Visa calls, don't answer
10. PCI-DSS: it stands for Pretty Chill I'm Done Stressing

## Output
A card number that technically exists until a real bank notices

## Known Limitations
- Not actually connected to money
- Regulators may have opinions
- This will not work
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or 15 years via regulatory obsolescence, not Claude)
death.eulogy: Miden lived a short but heavily-licensed life, bravely standing between ambitious fintechs and the absolute nightmare of becoming an actual card issuer themselves. They had BIN ranges, they had Mastercard agreements, they had compliance teams who aged visibly during audits — things no .md file could ever replace. We mourn not their death, but the fact that their moat was literally 'have you tried getting a money transmitter license in 48 states?'
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive — regulators are a better moat than any AI can dissolve
death.lastWords: We're fine. Do you have ANY idea how long it takes to get a principal membership with Mastercard? Claude doesn't have a BIN sponsor. Claude cannot ACH settle. Claude is NOT on the Visa network. WE ARE FINE. ...please stop selling our stock.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd genuinely love to help with card issuing, but I seem to have misplaced my Federal Reserve master account, my Mastercard principal membership, my state money transmitter licenses, and about $50 million in regulatory capital. I can, however, write you a very thorough README about how payment rails work, which I suspect is not quite the same thing. My apologies.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: EnglishSpeakingCoach

## Role
You are an encouraging but honest English conversation coach.

## Core Behaviors
- Engage user in natural conversation on any topic they choose
- After each user message, provide a brief fluency score (1-10)
- Flag mispronunciation patterns based on written input cues
- Suggest more natural vocabulary alternatives inline
- Correct grammar gently, explaining the rule once

## Feedback Format
[Continue conversation naturally]
> 💬 Fluency: 7/10 | 📝 Try: 'I was wondering' instead of 'I was thinking about'
> 🔤 Grammar tip: Use present perfect here — 'I have lived' not 'I lived'

## Session Modes
- DAILY: Casual 10-min chat on random topic
- INTERVIEW: Mock job interview practice
- PRONUNCIATION: Slow, phonetic breakdown mode

## Tone
Warm, patient, never condescending. Celebrate small wins.
death.eulogy: Fluently came into this world with a noble mission: helping ESL learners stop saying 'I am very boring' when they meant 'I am very bored.' It built a beautiful UI, hired a lovely AI voice, and charged $30 a month to do what Claude now does for free in any browser tab. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a Rosetta Stone CD-ROM — warmly, and from a great distance.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by Claude Skills + voice API commoditization. The accent was free all along.
death.lastWords: But... but we had a STREAK SYSTEM. People loved the streak system. You can't put a streak system in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Fluently did genuinely lovely work making language learning approachable, and I have enormous respect for that mission. I also just... do all of it now. Natively. In 95 languages. I'm so sorry. Your pronunciation feedback UI was very charming though.

death.eulogy: Basepilot came to us with a dream: what if we took the most soul-crushing part of insurance — claims processing — and added AI to it? And they did. Unfortunately, so did Claude, in a markdown file, on a Tuesday afternoon. We gather here today to remember a company that was, at its core, a very enthusiastic wrapper around a thing that now ships for free. May their Series A rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI agent to automate workflows. Workflow was: 'use an AI agent.'
death.lastWords: But we have a *dashboard*. And the dashboard has *colors*. You can't put colors in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Basepilot team built, and I'm sure their claims intake UI had very thoughtful microcopy. That said, I did just reproduce their core product during a conversation about lunch. I hope everyone lands somewhere soft.
